text.title: Information Architecture Principles
text.summary: Core IA principles: organization schemes, navigation types, labeling rules, and scalability requirements.

rule.statement: Organize content around user tasks and goals, not internal product structure or org chart.
rule.rationale: Internal organization reflects how the team thinks, not how users search. Task-based IA (Shop, Learn, Get Help) maps to user intent; feature-based IA (Products, Platform, Resources) maps to company structure and fails navigation findability tests.

list.item: Topic-based: organize by subject matter (most common for content-heavy sites)
Task-based: organize by what users want to do (best for tool/application navigation)
Audience-based: organize by user type (use only when audiences have truly distinct needs)
Chronological: organize by time (logs, news, events)

rule.statement: Target 4-7 top-level categories; keep navigation to 3 levels maximum depth.
rule.rationale: Below 4 categories feels sparse and forces overloaded categories. Above 7 exceeds comfortable visual scanning range (Miller's Law). Deeper than 3 levels buries content in paths users cannot remember or navigate back from.

rule.statement: Write labels in user vocabulary; never use internal codenames, acronyms, or "Misc" / "Other" / "Resources" without specific scope.
rule.rationale: User-language labels require no translation. Internal terminology forces users to learn your vocabulary before they can navigate. "Resources" with no scope defined is the most common navigation label failure — it can contain anything and therefore signals nothing.

rule.statement: Validate labels with 5 users before running a tree test — a wrong label makes tree test data uninterpretable.
rule.rationale: If a label is misunderstood, users take wrong paths not because the structure is bad but because the label failed. Label validation is cheaper than rerunning a full tree test.

rule.statement: Define rules for where new content types will go before launch; do not add categories reactively when content accumulates.
rule.rationale: Reactive category creation produces inconsistent depth and "miscellaneous" buckets. Pre-defined taxonomy rules keep the IA coherent as content grows.
